_comment: Curated illustrative examples for the Securities use-case core concepts. Real-world capital-markets instances (general domain knowledge), not FIBO axioms. Applied by etl/to_okf.py when FIBO supplies none; provenance curated.

examples.title: security
examples: A share of Apple Inc. common stock, or a US Treasury note.

examples.title: debt instrument
examples: A 10-year US Treasury note paying semi-annual interest.

examples.title: tradable debt instrument
examples: A 5-year corporate note that is listed and can be bought or sold on an exchange.

examples.title: equity instrument
examples: A common share of Microsoft Corporation.

examples.title: share
examples: One share of Tesla, Inc. common stock.

examples.title: listed security
examples: Apple Inc. (AAPL) common stock, listed on Nasdaq.

examples.title: security form
examples: Registered form (the issuer records the holder) versus bearer form (whoever holds the certificate owns it).

examples.title: bond
examples: A US$1,000 face-value, 10-year US Treasury bond paying a 4% coupon.

examples.title: government bond
examples: A German Bund or a US Treasury bond.

examples.title: corporate bond
examples: A 30-year bond issued by AT&T Inc. to fund network investment.

examples.title: municipal bond
examples: A general-obligation bond issued by the State of California to fund infrastructure.

examples.title: zero coupon bond
examples: A US Treasury STRIPS that pays no coupon and trades at a discount to its face value.

examples.title: fixed coupon bond
examples: A 5-year bond paying a fixed 3.5% annual coupon.

examples.title: convertible bond
examples: A Tesla convertible note the holder can convert into a set number of Tesla shares.

examples.title: callable bond
examples: A corporate bond the issuer may redeem after five years if interest rates fall.

examples.title: floating rate note
examples: A 3-year note paying SOFR + 0.50%, resetting quarterly.

examples.title: commercial paper
examples: 90-day unsecured commercial paper issued by a large corporation to fund working capital.

examples.title: treasury bill
examples: A 13-week US Treasury bill sold at a discount to its US$1,000 face value.

examples.title: bond coupon
examples: The semi-annual 2% interest payment on a bond with a 4% annual coupon.

examples.title: sovereign debt instrument
examples: A Japanese Government Bond (JGB) issued by the government of Japan.

examples.title: common share
examples: One ordinary share of Amazon.com, Inc. carrying one vote.

examples.title: preferred share
examples: A 5% preferred share paying a fixed dividend ahead of common shareholders.

examples.title: non-voting share
examples: A Class C Alphabet share (GOOG), which carries no voting rights.

examples.title: partially paid share
examples: A share issued where only part of the subscription price has been called and paid.

examples.title: depositary receipt
examples: A receipt representing shares of a foreign company held by a depositary bank.

examples.title: American depositary receipt
examples: An ADR representing shares of Toyota, traded on a US exchange in US dollars.

examples.title: global depositary receipt
examples: A GDR representing shares of an Indian company, traded on the London Stock Exchange.

examples.title: dividend
examples: A quarterly cash dividend of US$0.24 per share declared by a company's board.

examples.title: fixed rate dividend
examples: A preferred share's fixed 6% annual dividend.

examples.title: share yield
examples: A US$2 annual dividend on a US$50 share, a 4% dividend yield.

examples.title: asset-backed security
examples: A security backed by a pool of auto loans or credit-card receivables.

examples.title: mortgage-backed security
examples: A Ginnie Mae pass-through backed by a pool of residential mortgages.

examples.title: collateralized debt obligation
examples: A CDO whose tranches are backed by a portfolio of corporate bonds and loans.

examples.title: tranche
examples: The senior AAA-rated tranche of a securitization, paid before the junior tranches.

examples.title: pool
examples: A pool of residential mortgages underlying a mortgage-backed security.

examples.title: m b s pool
examples: The pool of conforming 30-year mortgages backing a Fannie Mae MBS.

examples.title: c d o pool
examples: The portfolio of leveraged loans backing a collateralized loan obligation.

examples.title: collective investment vehicle
examples: A UCITS fund that pools money from many investors.

examples.title: mutual fund
examples: The Vanguard 500 Index Fund, an open-end fund tracking the S&P 500.

examples.title: exchange-traded fund
examples: SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), traded intraday on an exchange.

examples.title: hedge fund
examples: A long/short equity hedge fund open only to accredited investors.

examples.title: equity fund
examples: A large-cap equity fund that invests primarily in common shares.

examples.title: fund unit
examples: One unit of a mutual fund, priced at its net asset value (NAV).

examples.title: issuer
examples: Apple Inc., the issuer of AAPL shares and its corporate bonds.

examples.title: share issuer
examples: A corporation that issues common shares to raise equity capital.

examples.title: m b s issuer
examples: A government-sponsored enterprise such as Fannie Mae issuing mortgage-backed securities.

examples.title: security underwriter
examples: An investment bank that buys a new bond issue from the issuer to resell to investors.

examples.title: underwriter
examples: The lead investment bank in an IPO syndicate.

examples.title: security underwriting arrangement
examples: A firm-commitment underwriting agreement for an initial public offering.

examples.title: prospectus
examples: The prospectus filed with the SEC for an initial public offering.

examples.title: international securities identification number
examples: US0378331005, the ISIN for Apple Inc. common stock.

examples.title: security registry
examples: A registrar maintaining the register of a company's shareholders.

examples.title: listing
examples: Apple Inc.'s listing on the Nasdaq Stock Market under ticker AAPL.

examples.title: basket of securities
examples: The basket of 500 stocks underlying an S&P 500 index fund.

examples.title: portfolio
examples: A diversified portfolio of 60% equities and 40% bonds.

examples.title: weighted average maturity
examples: The 18-month weighted-average maturity of a money-market fund's holdings.

examples.title: redemption schedule
examples: A sinking-fund schedule that redeems 10% of a bond issue each year.

examples.title: redemption event
examples: A bond reaching maturity and repaying principal to its holders.

examples.title: yield
examples: A bond bought at US$950 that pays US$40 a year has roughly a 4.2% current yield.
